nativeRange.habitat: High-altitude ponds, rivers, and streams; notably the Anisakan Falls pond near Pyin Oo Lwin and the lower Chindwin River drainage, Mandalay Division
nativeRange.biotope: Heavily vegetated still or slow-moving water with dark substrate, leaf litter, and submerged plants

diet.notes: The Odessa Barb readily accepts good-quality flakes and pellets, and benefits from occasional live or frozen foods such as bloodworm, Daphnia, and brine shrimp, which tend to bring out the best coloration in males. The species has also been known to graze on vegetable matter.

avoidWith: small invertebrates that may be outcompeted at feeding

summary: The Odessa Barb (Pethia padamya) is a schooling barb from Myanmar valued for the vivid red flanks males develop, especially during courtship. Peaceful and generally considered hardy, it does best in groups of six or more and tolerates cooler water than most tropical barbs.
careNotes: The Odessa Barb was formerly classified as Puntius padamya before being moved to the genus Pethia following a 2007 generic revision; the older name Puntius padamya is still encountered in older aquarium literature. Males develop bright red horizontal banding over iridescent scales edged in black, a coloration that tends to intensify during courtship displays and become less vivid at rest. Females are less colorful overall and carry a more rounded body shape. The Odessa Barb is generally considered less prone to fin-nipping than the Tiger Barb, though individual variation exists, and long-finned tankmates such as guppies, bettas, and angelfish are still best avoided. The species tends to be active and fast-moving in the mid-water, which can put slower or more timid fish at a disadvantage during feeding. Wild populations are restricted to a small area of Myanmar's Mandalay Division, including an artificial pond near Anisakan Falls and the lower Chindwin River drainage — a naturally limited range for a fish that is now widely kept in the hobby. The species prefers some water movement and well-oxygenated conditions. Temperature tolerance extends cooler than most tropical barbs, with reports of the species tolerating water as cool as 16°C, which can make it suitable for unheated aquariums in temperate climates.
breedingNotes: The Odessa Barb is an egg-scattering spawner that provides no parental care. Males pursue females vigorously during courtship, and females may scatter 150–200 adhesive eggs among fine-leaved plants. Parents will readily eat their own eggs if left in the tank, so breeders generally remove the adults immediately after spawning, or use a separate breeding tank with a spawning grid or dense planting to protect the eggs. Fry hatch in approximately 24–48 hours and become free-swimming a few days later. Initial foods are infusoria or fine powder fry food, transitioning to baby brine shrimp as the fry grow.

additional.notes: Cross-checked lifespan (3–5 years), temperature (23–26°C), diet, and breeding notes. Size stated as 3–4 inches which is on the high end; deferred to SeriouslyFish and FishBase for size.

fish.breedingNotes: The Odessa Barb is an egg-scatterer that provides no parental care and will readily consume its own eggs if left in the breeding tank. A separate breeding setup with fine-leaved plants or a spawning grid helps protect the eggs until the adults can be removed. Fry hatch in approximately 24–48 hours and initially require infusoria or fine powder fry food, transitioning to baby brine shrimp as they grow.
